title: A visit to 3-manifolds in the quest to understand random Galois groups
speaker: Melanie Matchett Wood
abstract: Cohen, Lenstra, and Martinet gave conjectural distributions for the class group of a random number field. Since the class group is the Galois group of the maximum abelian unramified extension, a natural generalization would be to give a conjecture for the distribution of the Galois group of the maximal unramified extension. Previous work (joint with Liu and Zurieck-Brown) produced a plausible conjecture for the part of this Galois group relatively prime to the number of roots of unity in the base field. There is a deep analogy between number fields and 3-manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic-to-topological motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from Cohen-Lenstra class-group distributions, rewrites class groups as abelianizations of unramified Galois groups, and uses function-field and three-manifold analogies to motivate distributions of nonabelian profinite fundamental groups.

proof_stages.label: Dunfield-Thurston random 3-manifold model
proof_stages.summary: A random closed oriented 3-manifold is produced by gluing two genus-g handlebodies via a random mapping class, taking word length to infinity, and then taking genus to infinity so that all 3-manifolds are eventually seen.

proof_stages.label: Profinite state space via levels
proof_stages.summary: The space of small profinite groups is topologized by finitely generated formations of finite groups, with each level giving a canonical finite pro-C quotient and basic open sets specified by that quotient.

proof_stages.label: Explicit limiting distribution and support
proof_stages.summary: The main result gives explicit limiting probabilities for each finite-level quotient condition, hence a limiting distribution on profinite completions together with an explicit support and concrete quotient statistics.

proof_stages.label: Moment strategy from surjection counts
proof_stages.summary: The proof computes moments, namely expected numbers of surjections from π1(M) to finite groups, and uses a general moment-to-distribution theory when the moments are sufficiently controlled.

proof_stages.label: Orientation and cohomological restrictions
proof_stages.summary: Because the raw moments are too large, the argument refines groups by an orientation class in third group homology and imposes 3-manifold restrictions coming from Poincare duality and parity for cohomology with irreducible finite-field coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Restricted support, density, and existence
proof_stages.summary: Within the class satisfying the orientation and cohomological restrictions, the moments determine the distribution; every admissible open set has positive Dunfield-Thurston probability, giving density and existence of 3-manifolds with prescribed finite-quotient behavior.

examples.name: Cohen-Lenstra class groups
examples.note: The classical question about distributions of class groups is used to generate the nonabelian analogue by viewing the class group as the abelianization of the Galois group of the maximal unramified extension, with roots of unity foreshadowing the later orientation issue.
examples.engagement_reason: Cohen-Lenstra class groups are introduced as motivating distributions/conjectures and later compared to the manifold distribution, but no specific class group distribution is computed or derived.

examples.name: Function fields as curves over finite fields
examples.note: Finite extensions of Fq(t) are recalled as curves mapping to P1, making the unramified Galois group into a fundamental group and motivating the three-dimensional topological analogue.
examples.engagement_reason: Function fields are described as analogous to curves over finite fields with fundamental groups and homology, but the speaker does not work through a concrete function-field example.
